title: Joshua 11
subtitle: The Northern Campaign: Hazor Destroyed

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Conquest of Canaan

sections.header: Verses 1–15 — Jabin's Coalition Defeated at the Waters of Merom

heb.paragraph: A key Hebrew term in this section on northern coalition; chariots hamstrung; Hazor burned carries theological weight that illuminates the passage's role in Joshua's larger narrative of conquest and covenant fulfilment.

refs.note: Moses' charge to Israel — "be strong and courageous" — provides the theological foundation for every episode in Joshua. The conquest is the practical outworking of Deuteronomy's promises and warnings.
The NT reads Joshua's conquest as typological — a partial fulfilment that points to the greater rest available in Christ.

notes.note: MacArthur: The passage on northern coalition; chariots hamstrung; Hazor burned reveals God's character in action — not as abstract theology but as concrete intervention in Israel's history. Every battle, every boundary, every judgment call demonstrates that YHWH is a God who acts, not merely a God who speaks.

MacArthur: Joshua's leadership here reflects the qualities God demanded in chapter 1 — strength, courage, and Torah-obedience. The conquest succeeds not because of military superiority but because of covenantal faithfulness.

MacArthur: The theological lesson is transferable: God's people in every era face situations that require the same trust in divine promises that Joshua demonstrates here. The form of the battle changes; the source of victory does not.

MacArthur: The NT reads these OT events as types and shadows (1 Cor 10:6,11). What Joshua accomplished physically — leading God's people into their inheritance — Christ accomplishes spiritually, leading believers into the fullness of God's promises.

Calvin: On northern coalition; chariots hamstrung; Hazor burned: God's actions here demonstrate that he is both sovereign over the nations and faithful to his particular covenant with Israel. The universal Lord works through specific historical events to accomplish his redemptive purposes.

Calvin: The practical application extends beyond ancient Israel — every generation of God's people faces its own "Canaan" of challenges that require the same faith, obedience, and dependence on divine power demonstrated in this passage.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET Note: The Hebrew syntax in this section employs thewayyiqtolnarrative chain characteristic of historical prose. Key terms carry specific covenant and military connotations that anchor the passage in the broader Deuteronomistic theological vocabulary.

NET Note: Textual variants in the LXX for this passage suggest the Greek translators worked from a Hebrew Vorlage that occasionally diverged from the MT, particularly in geographic details and proper names.

hess.source: Richard S. Hess, Joshua, Tyndale OT Commentary (1996) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Hess: The archaeological and textual evidence for northern coalition; chariots hamstrung; Hazor burned in this section aligns with Late Bronze Age Canaanite material culture. The narrative details — toponyms, military tactics, territorial descriptions — reflect authentic local knowledge consistent with an early source.

Hess: The literary structure here follows conventions of ancient Near Eastern conquest accounts, where the divine warrior fights on behalf of the vassal army. The theological framing is distinctively Israelite but the narrative genre is internationally attested.

howard.source: David M. Howard Jr., Joshua, New American Commentary (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Howard: The canonical placement of this passage on northern coalition; chariots hamstrung; Hazor burned within the larger Joshua narrative serves a literary function: it advances the theme of covenant fulfilment and demonstrates the pattern of divine initiative followed by human obedience (or failure).

Howard: The keyword repetitions and structural echoes link this passage to the conquest's overarching theological argument: God keeps his promises, and Israel's response determines whether they experience blessing or judgment within that framework.

hist.context: This section addresses northern coalition; chariots hamstrung; Hazor burned. The narrative advances the book's central argument: God is faithful to his promise of land, and Israel's response — obedience or disobedience — determines how they experience that faithfulness within their generation.

sections.header: Verses 16–23 — Summary: Joshua Took the Whole Land

heb.paragraph: A key Hebrew term in this section on conquest summary; the land had rest from war carries theological weight that illuminates the passage's role in Joshua's larger narrative of conquest and covenant fulfilment.

refs.note: Moses' charge to Israel — "be strong and courageous" — provides the theological foundation for every episode in Joshua. The conquest is the practical outworking of Deuteronomy's promises and warnings.
The NT reads Joshua's conquest as typological — a partial fulfilment that points to the greater rest available in Christ.

notes.note: MacArthur: On 23 — Summary: Joshua Took the Whole Land: he greatest leader Israel has ever known is dead. Yet God's program does not pause. The death of a leader is never the death of the mission. God buries his workers but carries on his work. MacArthur emphasizes the doctrinal significance of this passage within the framework of systematic theology. The text establishes principles that remain normative for the church — not merely descriptive of ancient Israel but prescriptive for how God's people should understand His character and respond in obedience.

Calvin: On 23 — Summary: Joshua Took the Whole Land: conditional — but it is given to a man who must still fight. God's sovereignty does not eliminate human effort; it guarantees its success when exercised in obedience. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The summary statement 'Joshua took this entire land' (v.16) uses the verb lākaḏ ('to capture, seize'), which NET translators note refers to military control rather than settlement or occupation. The qualifying phrase 'for a long time' (yāmîm rabbîm, v.18) literally means 'many days' and is deliberately imprecise — the text avoids specifying the campaign's duration, though later tradition (based on Caleb's age in 14:10) estimates approximately seven years.

The Anakites (ʿănāqîm) surviving in Gaza, Gath, and Ashdod (v.22) — later Philistine cities — provides the NET translators with a significant narrative connection: Goliath of Gath (1 Samuel 17) may descend from these remnant Anakite populations. The term 'rest' (nāḥ) in v.23 carries covenantal significance, echoing Deuteronomy's promise of 'rest from all your enemies' (Deuteronomy 12:10) and signaling that the conquest has achieved its theological objective.

hess.source: Richard S. Hess, Joshua, Tyndale OT Commentary (1996) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Hess reads the summary statement — 'Joshua took the entire land' — in light of the qualifying phrases that surround it: 'Joshua waged war against all these kings for a long time' (v.18) and the Anakites survived in Gaza, Gath, and Ashdod (v.22). The summary uses conventional ANE conquest rhetoric where 'the entire land' means political control rather than occupation of every square meter. The note that 'the land had rest from war' (v.23) parallels Judges' cyclical rest-language and signals a structural transition from conquest to settlement.

howard.source: David M. Howard Jr., Joshua, New American Commentary (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Howard: On 23 — Summary: Joshua Took the Whole Land: strong, v.6), C (meditate on Torah, v.7-8), B' (command to be strong, v.9a), A' (promise of presence, v.9b). The Torah stands at the centre — it is the pivot around which both courage and presence rotate.

hist.context: This section addresses conquest summary; the land had rest from war. The narrative advances the book's central argument: God is faithful to his promise of land, and Israel's response — obedience or disobedience — determines how they experience that faithfulness within their generation.

ppl.name: Joshua
ppl.text: Joshua leads Israel through the events of chapter 11, demonstrating the leadership qualities God demanded in 1:6-9: strength, courage, and Torah-obedience.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVJoshua did to them as the Lord had directed: He hamstrung their horses and burned their chariots.ESVAnd Joshua did to them just as the LORD said to him: he hamstrung their horses and burned their chariots with fire.NIVAll these kings joined forces and made camp together at the Waters of Merom to fight against Israel.ESVAnd all these kings joined their forces and came and encamped together at the waters of Merom to fight against Israel.

src.title: Amarna Letters (EA 285-290)
src.quote: Letters from Canaanite vassal kings to Egyptian Pharaoh describe “Habiru” invaders destabilising city-states.
src.note: The Amarna correspondence (c.1350 BC) describes social upheaval in Canaan that some scholars correlate with the Israelite conquest. The “Habiru” are not identical to “Hebrews” but may include them.

src.title: Merneptah Stele (c.1207 BC)
src.quote: “Israel is laid waste; his seed is not.”
src.note: The earliest extra-biblical reference to Israel as a people group in Canaan. The determinative marks Israel as a people, not a territory — consistent with a recently settled group.

src.title: Papyrus Anastasi I
src.quote: An Egyptian satirical letter describing Canaan's geography, roads, and fortified cities.
src.note: The detailed Canaanite geography matches Joshua's territorial descriptions, confirming the text's familiarity with the region's actual landscape.

rec.text: Origen reads Joshua 11 christologically: every victory is a type of Christ's victory over sin and death. The conquest of Canaan foreshadows the conquest of the heart by the gospel.

rows.label: Jabin's Coalition Defeated at the Waters of Merom — 11:1-(15, '')
Resolution
rows.text: The narrative advances through divine action and human response

hebtext.note: A key Hebrew term in this section on northern coalition; chariots hamstrung; Hazor burned carries theological weight that illuminates the passage's role in Joshua's larger narrative of conquest and covenant fulfilment.

A key Hebrew term in this section on conquest summary; the land had rest from war carries theological weight that illuminates the passage's role in Joshua's larger narrative of conquest and covenant fulfilment.

thread.text: The events of Joshua 11 fulfil what Moses promised and commanded in his farewell. The conquest is Deuteronomy's theology enacted in history.
Joshua 11 contributes to the partial rest that the NT identifies as incomplete — awaiting its fulfilment in Christ.

tx.title: Shorter text form
tx.content: The LXX of Joshua is significantly shorter than the MT, especially in chapters 5-6, 20, and 24. Some scholars argue the LXX preserves an earlier, more concise text.
tx.note: The LXX order and shorter readings may witness to a pre-Deuteronomistic edition of Joshua.

tx.title: Altar-building placement
tx.content: A Qumran fragment places the Ebal altar episode (Josh 8:30-35) after 5:1, supporting the LXX order rather than the MT.
tx.note: This variant supports the view that the MT order was editorially rearranged, possibly to group all conquest narratives together.

themes.note: Joshua 11: the theme of faithful promise-fulfilment continues as God gives what he promised and Israel responds with varying degrees of obedience.

coaching.tip: The summary statement — 'So Joshua took the entire land... Then the land had rest from war' — creates a theological tension that the rest of Judges will expose. The land was 'taken' in principle but not fully possessed in practice. Joshua's conquest is complete and incomplete simultaneously.

prayer_prompt: The northern campaign completed what God had promised, systematically and thoroughly. Consider asking God about the thorough obedience He requires — what you've left half-done.
